source.definition: to fall as small balls or lumps of ice from the sky during certain weather conditions

explanation.sourceText: The verb "hail" originates from Old English "hagol" or "hægol", which referred to small pellets of frozen rain.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „hail” provine din engleza veche „hagol” sau „hægol”, care desemnau mici bile sau bucăți de ploaie înghețată.

source.additional: The word is commonly used to describe the meteorological phenomenon of hail, which typically occurs during thunderstorms.

target.definition: o formă de precipitații în care bucăți de gheață cad din cer

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian phrase "a cădea grindină" is an internal formation from the verb "a cădea" (from Latin "cadere", to fall) and the noun "grindină" (from Latin "grandinā" for hail).
explanation.targetText: Expresia românească „a cădea grindină” este formată intern, din verbul „a cădea” (din latină „cadere”, a cădea) și substantivul „grindină” (din latină „grandinā”, grindină).

target.additional: Wird oft im Kontext von Wetterphänomenen verwendet.

explanation.sourceText: Hail is a specific type of precipitation.
explanation.targetText: Grindina este o formă specială de precipitații.

explanation.sourceText: Used to refer to the weather phenomenon of hail.
explanation.targetText: Ca în germană, pentru fenomenul meteorologic.

examples.sourceText: It has been hailing all day today.
It has been hailing all day today.

learningTips.text: Remember the English word "hail" by thinking of tiny ice balls hitting your car windshield during a storm.
Connect the Romanian word "grindină" with the English word "grind" to imagine the rough texture of hailstones.
Think of the phrase "hail to the king" but replace 'king' with 'storm' to remember hail as a weather event.
Practice sentences like "It will hail tomorrow" to get comfortable using the verb in context.
